epigraph.text: For we are God's handiwork, created in Christ Jesus to do good works, which God prepared in advance for us to do.

content.text:
Real-world shapes are rarely simple rectangles or perfect circles. 
Buildings, gardens, lakes, and artwork typically combine multiple 
geometric forms. In this section, we learn systematic strategies 
for calculating the areas of such **composite figures**—shapes 
made up of two or more simpler shapes.
There are two main approaches to finding the area of a composite figure:

content.title: Addition Method
content.content:
**Addition:** Divide the figure into non-overlapping simple shapes, 
calculate each area separately, and add them together.

content.title: Subtraction Method
content.content:
**Subtraction:** Enclose the figure in a larger simple shape 
(like a rectangle), then subtract the areas of the "extra" regions.

content.text: Both methods work; choose whichever seems simpler for the given figure.
When facing a composite figure, follow these steps:

items: **Analyze** the figure. Identify its component shapes.
**Decide** whether to use addition or subtraction.

**Find** all necessary dimensions (you may need to 
 calculate some from given information).
**Combine** the areas (add or subtract as appropriate).

content.title: L-shaped room

content.text:
Many composite figures include circles or parts of circles (semicircles, 
quarter circles, etc.).

content.title: Semicircle on a rectangle
content.solution:
The rectangle has dimensions $10 \times 8$, so its area is 80.

The semicircle has diameter 8 (the width of the rectangle), so 
radius $r = 4$. A semicircle's area is half a full circle:
\[
 A_{\text{semicircle}} = \frac{1}{2}\pi r^2 = \frac{1}{2}\pi(4)^2 = 8\pi
\]

Total area:
\[
 A = 80 + 8\pi \approx 80 + 25.13 \approx 105.13 \text{ square units}
\]

content.title: Rectangle with circular hole
content.solution:
Rectangle area: $A_{\text{rect}} = 20 \times 15 = 300$ cm²

Circle area (with $r = 3$): $A_{\text{circle}} = \pi(3)^2 = 9\pi$ cm²

Remaining area:
\[
 A = 300 - 9\pi \approx 300 - 28.27 \approx 271.73 \text{ cm}^2
\]

content.title: Running track
content.solution:
The figure consists of:

- A rectangle: $100 \times 40 = 4000$ m²

- Two semicircles (together forming one full circle) with 
 diameter 40 m, so radius $r = 20$ m: $\pi(20)^2 = 400\pi$ m²

Total area:
\[
 A = 4000 + 400\pi \approx 4000 + 1256.64 \approx 5256.64 \text{ m}^2
\]

content.title: House shape
content.solution:
Rectangle area: $A_{\text{rect}} = 8 \times 5 = 40$

Triangle area (base 8, height 3): 
$A_{\text{tri}} = \frac{1}{2}(8)(3) = 12$

Total area:
\[
 A = 40 + 12 = 52 \text{ square units}
\]

content.title: Arrow shape
content.solution:
The arrow consists of:

- A rectangle (the "shaft"): $6 \times 4 = 24$

- A triangle (the "point"): base $8$, height $4$, 
 so $\frac{1}{2}(8)(4) = 16$

Total area:
\[
 A = 24 + 16 = 40 \text{ square units}
\]

content.title: Hexagon as triangles
content.solution:
The hexagon divides into 6 equilateral triangles, each with 
side length 6.

Area of one equilateral triangle:
\[
 A_{\triangle} = \frac{s^2\sqrt{3}}{4} = \frac{6^2\sqrt{3}}{4} = \frac{36\sqrt{3}}{4} = 9\sqrt{3}
\]

Total area:
\[
 A = 6 \times 9\sqrt{3} = 54\sqrt{3} \approx 93.53
\]

content.title: Shaded region between circles
content.solution:
Large circle area: $\pi(10)^2 = 100\pi$

Small circle area: $\pi(6)^2 = 36\pi$

Shaded area:
\[
 A = 100\pi - 36\pi = 64\pi \approx 201.06
\]

content.title: Circle inscribed in square
content.solution:
The diameter of the inscribed circle equals the side of the 
square, so the square has side $2(5) = 10$.

Square area: $10^2 = 100$

Circle area: $\pi(5)^2 = 25\pi$

Shaded area:
\[
 A = 100 - 25\pi \approx 100 - 78.54 \approx 21.46
\]

content.title: Square inscribed in circle
content.solution:
The diagonal of the inscribed square equals the diameter of 
the circle: $d = 2(10) = 20$.

For a square with diagonal $d$, the side is $s = \frac{d}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{20}{\sqrt{2}} = 10\sqrt{2}$.

Square area: $s^2 = (10\sqrt{2})^2 = 200$

Circle area: $\pi(10)^2 = 100\pi$

Shaded area:
\[
 A = 100\pi - 200 \approx 314.16 - 200 = 114.16
\]

content.text:
Finding the perimeter of a composite figure requires identifying 
and measuring all the *outer* boundary segments.

content.title: Track perimeter
content.problem:
Find the perimeter of the running track from Example [ex:running-track] 
(rectangle 100 m by 40 m with semicircles at each end).
content.solution:
The perimeter consists of:

- Two straight sides of 100 m each: $2(100) = 200$ m

- Two semicircles (forming one full circle) with 
 diameter 40, so circumference $\pi d = 40\pi$ m

Total perimeter:
\[
 P = 200 + 40\pi \approx 200 + 125.66 \approx 325.66 \text{ m}
\]

marginNotes.text:
Commit to the Lord whatever you do, 
and he will establish your plans.

By the grace God has given me, 
I laid a foundation as a wise builder, and someone else is building 
on it.

Do you not know that in a race 
all the runners run, but only one gets the prize? Run in such a 
way as to get the prize.

There is a time for everything, 
and a season for every activity under the heavens.
